project.name: personal-portfolio
project.description: Dark-themed personal portfolio built with Astro 5 and React 19 islands showcasing 20 interactive demos, with three-locale (EN/ES/CA) i18next i18n, Sentry observability, sharded accessibility CI, and a FastAPI planner backend. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

nodes.summary: Filters candidate regions down to those whose shape matches plate-like proportions.

nodes.name: clearBorder
nodes.summary: Removes connected components touching the image border from a binary mask.

nodes.name: fillHoles
nodes.summary: Fills interior holes in a binary mask via flood fill to solidify character blobs.

nodes.name: getRegions
nodes.summary: Extracts connected-component regions and their bounding boxes from a binary mask.

nodes.name: adaptiveThreshold
nodes.summary: Applies adaptive thresholding to a grayscale image with a tunable sensitivity parameter.

nodes.name: findPlate
nodes.summary: Locates the most plate-like region in the source image and returns its cropped mat.

nodes.name: findChars
nodes.summary: Segments individual character glyphs from the cropped plate image.

nodes.name: loadImageAsMat
nodes.summary: Loads an image source into an OpenCV mat ready for processing.

nodes.name: prepareTemplates
nodes.summary: Builds normalized character templates from a font atlas for later template matching.

nodes.name: identifyChars
nodes.summary: Matches segmented character mats against templates to read the plate string.

nodes.name: runPipeline
nodes.summary: Top-level entry point that runs the full plate-detection pipeline and returns the recognized characters with debug stages.

tour.title: Project Overview & Onboarding
tour.description: Start here to learn what the project is: a dark-themed Astro 5 + React 19 portfolio that hosts 20 interactive demos in three locales (EN/ES/CA) and deploys to GitHub Pages. The README lays out the stack, the make-based workflows, the demo catalogue, and the testing layers, while AGENTS.md distills the same architecture into the conventions and pitfalls an agent must respect. Together they form the roadmap for every step that follows.

tour.title: Entry Point & File-Based Routing
tour.description: Astro turns files in src/pages into routes, so the URL structure IS the page structure. The root index.astro simply redirects to the default localized home, [lang]/index.astro renders the actual landing page per language, and the dynamic [lang]/demos/[demo].astro resolves a slug + locale to the right demo page. This is the project's front door and the reason i18n routing (/, /es/, /ca/) works without a router library.
tour.languageLesson: Astro uses file-based routing: bracketed filenames like [lang] and [demo] become dynamic path parameters, and a page can statically generate every combination via getStaticPaths.

tour.title: Layout Shells
tour.description: Every route renders inside a layout. Layout.astro provides the HTML document shell — head metadata, global styles, and the pre-paint theme initialization — while DemoLayout.astro composes the demo-specific chrome (header, sidebar, content slot) on top of it. Understanding these two shells explains where the global concerns from Step 2 (theming, fonts, SEO) are actually wired in.

tour.title: Homepage Sections & Data Loading
tour.description: The landing page from Step 2 is assembled from a section registry: sections.ts maps each section id to its Astro component, Demos.astro renders the interactive-demos gallery, and loaders.ts pulls the portfolio's identity data (skills, projects, education, certifications, experience). This data-driven composition is why adding or reordering a homepage section is a config edit rather than a template rewrite.

tour.title: The Demo Registry
tour.description: A single source of truth drives all 20 demos. demos.json holds each demo's identity (slug, category, links, tech tags) that powers the gallery in Step 4, while demo-services.json plus its typed accessor demo-services.ts describe the dockerized backends (ports, stacks, health endpoints) shared by the dev orchestrator, the Makefile, live-demo tests, and Sentry tracing. Editing these JSON files — not scattered code — is how a demo is added or removed.

tour.title: A Representative Demo Island (CAIM)
tour.description: Demos are React islands hydrated client-side inside the demo pages. The CAIM information-retrieval demo is a good archetype: CAIMDemo.tsx frames localized copy and delegates to two lazily-loaded sub-tabs — PageRankTab.tsx, which computes airport PageRank and draws a D3 world map, and ZipfTab.tsx, which fits a Zipf curve over corpus word frequencies. This shows the standard pattern of a thin island shell splitting heavy visualizations into suspense-loaded subtabs.
tour.languageLesson: Astro islands hydrate selectively: client:visible defers a React component until it scrolls into view, and React.lazy + <Suspense> further code-splits heavy subtabs so the rest of the page stays interactive.

tour.title: Resilient Islands & Live Embeds
tour.description: Two cross-cutting components keep the demo grid robust. DemoErrorBoundary.tsx — one of the most depended-upon files in the codebase — wraps each island so a single crash never takes down the page, and LiveAppEmbed.tsx embeds a real backend in a sandboxed iframe, wiring the debug bridge and falling back to a mock when the backend is unreachable. These are the safety net and the bridge to the real applications behind the demos.

tour.title: Shared UI Chrome
tour.description: Beyond demos, the site has reusable chrome. Navbar.astro provides section anchors plus the theme toggle and language picker, Hero.astro renders the landing headline and call-to-action, ThemeToggle.astro switches and persists light/dark, and ThemeInit.astro runs an inline pre-paint script that restores the saved theme and design before the first frame to avoid a flash. This is the connective tissue wrapping every page from Step 3.

tour.title: Theming & Design System
tour.description: Theme and design are two independent axes, both token-based. themes.ts and designs.ts are the registries of selectable color themes and visual designs; themes.css and designs.css declare the CSS custom properties (light/dark, nord, solarized, comic, deco, …) that the toggles in Step 8 swap at runtime; and demo-theme.ts resolves those CSS variables into concrete color values for canvas and D3, where var() cannot be used directly. This is why demos must never hardcode hex colors.

tour.title: Internationalization
tour.description: All translatable copy lives in per-locale JSON catalogs, surfaced through a build-time i18n layer. locale-glob.ts — the single highest fan-in module — aggregates every per-locale namespace JSON (like locales/en/demos.json) into typed accessors, load.ts normalizes and repairs the loaded data (flattening dotted keys, fixing numeric-keyed objects from Crowdin), and locales.ts centralizes the supported/default locale configuration that drives the routing from Step 2. Parity tests later enforce that EN/ES/CA stay perfectly aligned.
tour.languageLesson: Vite's import.meta.glob eagerly imports many files at build time into one keyed map — here it stitches dozens of locale namespace JSONs into a typed translation tree with zero runtime fetches.

tour.title: Domain & Algorithm Libraries
tour.description: The demos are thin shells over framework-agnostic TypeScript logic in src/lib. wpgma.ts implements WPGMA hierarchical clustering, mpids.ts solves minimum positive-influence dominating sets, the jsbach interpreter.ts tokenizes and parses a custom esoteric language, and pagerank.ts is a pure iterative PageRank (the engine behind Step 6's CAIM tab). Keeping algorithms pure and UI-free makes them unit-testable in isolation and reusable across islands.

tour.title: FastAPI Planner Backend
tour.description: Most demos run fully in the browser, but the planificación demo needs a real PDDL planner, provided by a small in-repo service. main.py is a FastAPI app exposing a /plan endpoint backed by the ENHSP planner, its README documents local run, environment variables, and deployment, and the Dockerfile packages it into a slim Python image. This is the one server-side piece of the otherwise static site.
tour.languageLesson: FastAPI derives request/response validation and OpenAPI docs from Python type hints and Pydantic models, so declaring a typed handler signature is enough to get parsing, validation, and documentation for free.

tour.title: Testing Layers
tour.description: Quality is enforced by stacked test suites. content-parity.test.ts is a Vitest suite asserting the EN/ES/CA locale namespaces from Step 10 stay key- and order-aligned with the source data, while Playwright specs cover behavior in a real browser: portfolio-smoke.spec.ts loads the site and checks every section anchor, a11y.spec.ts runs axe-core accessibility scans across themes, and live-demos.spec.ts exercises the backend embeds while skipping gracefully when a backend is down. This layering catches both data drift and runtime regressions.

tour.title: Build, CI & Deployment
tour.description: Finally, the tooling that ties it all together. astro.config.mjs configures the static build and integrations, package.json declares the build/test scripts and dependencies, and the Makefile is the human-facing orchestration layer wrapping dev servers, Docker builds, and the full test matrix. In CI, test.yml runs lint, Vitest, and sharded Playwright suites, and deploy.yml builds the Astro site and publishes it to GitHub Pages on every push to main — closing the loop from local code to live site.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows use 'on' triggers to start jobs, run steps sequentially within a job, and parallelize via matrix/sharding — here the e2e suite is split into shards (a11y, browser, visual) that run concurrently to cut wall-clock CI time.
